EU Scrutinizes Google, YouTube, Apple, Snapchat Over Children's Online Safety
data.description:
By Edith Hancock


The European Union is looking into how well Alphabet's Google and YouTube, Apple and Snapchat protect minors online under the bloc's content-moderation law.

The executive arm of the EU, the European Commission, has asked for information on the companies' age-verification systems and on how they prevent minors from accessing products like drugs and vapes or from seeing harmful material such as content that could encourage eating disorders.

"We will do what it takes to ensure the physical and mental wellbeing of children and teens online," Henna Virkkunen, the EU's top tech enforcer, said in a statement Friday.

The watchdog asked Snapchat for information about how it prevents children younger than 13 from accessing its services and on the features it has in place to prevent the sale of illegal goods for children. It is also scrutinizing YouTube's age-assurance system and algorithms after reports of harmful content being disseminated to minors, and asking Apple and Google how they manage the risk of users being able to download harmful apps.

The EU's Digital Services Act requires companies like Google and Apple to do more to prevent minors being exposed to harmful and illegal content online. Companies can receive fines of up to 6% of their annual worldwide turnover for breaching the regulations.

"We have received the request from the commission and will collaborate to provide the necessary information," a Snapchat spokesperson said.

While requests for information are a routine part of the commission's law enforcement, officials can later open formal investigations into companies if they suspect breaches of the DSA.

Google said the company has worked with child-development experts to build age-appropriate experiences across Google, including on YouTube, with robust controls for parents. "We'll continue to engage with the commission on this critical area," it said.

Apple didn't immediately respond to a request for comment.


Write to Edith Hancock at edith.hancock@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

10-10-25 0842ET

Bristol Myers to Buy Orbital Therapeutics for $1.5 Billion
data.description:
By Colin Kellaher


Bristol Myers Squibb has agreed to buy privately held biotechnology company Orbital Therapeutics for $1.5 billion in cash in a deal that bolsters the drugmaker's cell-therapy portfolio.

Bristol Myers on Friday said Orbital's lead program, OTX-201, is an investigational next-generation CAR T-cell therapy designed to reprogram cells in vivo, with a potential best-in-class profile for autoimmune diseases.

CAR T, short for chimeric antigen receptor T-cell, is a complex treatment that works by manipulating a patient's own disease-fighting white blood cells--known as T cells--and then infusing them back into the body to fight cancer.

OTX-201 is a preclinical candidate currently in IND-enabling studies, Bristol Myers said.

The Princeton, N.J., company said the acquisition of Orbital and its next-generation RNA platform presents an opportunity to make CAR T-cell therapy more efficient and accessible to more patients.

Bristol Myers' Breyanzi, a CAR T-cell therapy approved in several lymphoma indications, generated worldwide revenue of $344 million in the second quarter of 2025.


Write to Colin Kellaher at colin.kellaher@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

10-10-25 0832ET

J&J: new data in neuropsychiatry
data.description:
Johnson & Johnson announces the presentation of 17 clinical and real-world studies at the annual meeting of the European College of Neuro-psychopharmacology (ECNP), to be held October 11-14 in Amsterdam. This work focuses on major depressive disorder (MDD), treatment-resistant depression (TRD), and schizophrenia.

Key findings include new Phase 3 analyses of the efficacy of lumateperone (CAPLYTA) and seltorexant in MDD, as well as data from the ESCAPE-TRD and ELLIPSE studies on esketamine nasal spray (SPRAVATO).

Bill Martin, Global Head of Neuroscience at Johnson & Johnson Innovative Medicine, emphasizes that the company is pursuing a "targeted, patient-centric" approach to delivering differentiated treatments for psychiatric disorders.
